summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Joinder – Application by wife for joinder of husband’s current wife in her personal capacity and as trustee of a discretionary trust – Where husband asserts that various cryptocurrency assets and transactions, including assets seized pursuant to a search order, relate not to his personal affairs but to business activities undertaken by him via the trust – Where husband alleges that orders made in the proceedings are impeding the business operations of the trust – Where wife asserts that the trust is a puppet of the husband or a sham designed to obscure the husband’s true financial circumstances – Where husband resigned as appointor of the trust but failed to disclose this fact – Where wife alleges that the husband’s wife has taken actions on his behalf for the purpose of defeating her claim behalf for the purpose of defeating her claim – Order for joinder made

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
The application presently before the Court is the wife's application to join the husband's partner, MS KARAMAZOV, as a party to the proceedings pursuant to rule 3.03(4) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). By Application in a Proceeding filed on 13 January 2026, the wife seeks to join Ms Karamazov both in her personal capacity and in her capacity as trustee of a discretionary trust known as the TT Trust.
The joinder application is opposed by the husband and by Ms Karamazov.
No documents were filed by or on behalf of Ms Karamazov despite what would appear to have been some attempt to do so, and the hearing proceeded on the basis that counsel appearing on behalf of Ms Karamazov took the Court through the relevant passages of the documents in question orally.

BACKGROUND

The husband and the wife were married in 2001 and separated on 14 February 2019. They have been engaged in long-running property settlement proceedings, which were commenced by the husband in what was then the Federal Circuit Court of Australia on 24 December 2020.
The major dispute between the parties relates to the extent of the husband's holdings in digital assets, particularly cryptocurrency, whether held directly by the husband or by entities under his control. The wife alleges serious deficiencies in the husband's disclosure in this regard.
On 19 June 2025, orders were made on the wife's application on an ex parte basis, which included search orders with respect to the husband's home and business premises, freezing orders and the appointment of a receiver with respect to digital assets.[1]
An independent forensic expert appointed pursuant to those orders has undertaken detailed and lengthy investigations into the husband's digital holdings, the process of which remains incomplete. The extent of the husband's compliance with the orders of 19 June 2025 is a matter of serious contention between the husband on the one hand and the wife (relying on information provided by the forensic expert) on the other.
Relevantly for the purposes of the present application, since those orders were made, the husband has asserted that some of the digital assets identified and/or seized as a consequence of execution of those orders were assets of the TT Trust and has asserted that the operation of those orders has had an impact on the trust and its operation of a business.
The wife challenges the legitimacy of the trust and alleges that it is a puppet of the husband or a sham concocted by him and Ms Karamazov to conceal his true financial circumstances.
The husband has deposed to the following:
(a) By way of affidavit filed on 4 July 2025:
...
I work for the trust ([TT Trust]). That work involves IT consulting, programming, and development of software. I raise the invoices for the trust.
The trust has advised on customer requested and data and those records are on various computers.
For example, the trust is providing services for a confidential [...] project and a software development project.
My spouse [Ms Karamazov] [sic] pays the rent and living expenses for the family from the trust and this is the way our family operates financially.
Due to the search, the trust money, which was held in a wallet, was transferred to the Receiver, and now, approximately $15,000.00 USD is now frozen. These funds are now needed immediately for continuity of the business and are essential for the payment of staff and contractor wages. In addition, these funds are essential for the payment of our own home rent which was due yesterday.

7. We do not have enough in any bank account to cover these immediate costs.

...
I believe that the search will mostly find information relating to the two companies in liquidation and the trust. The trust information is confidential and is not part of the orders.
I believe it is likely that many of the Devices that have been used in the last two or three years will contain confidential information on the trust.
I believe that I cooperated but of course at times I was frustrated and overwhelmed and worried about the trust’s business.[2]
(b) By way of affidavit filed on 15 August 2025:
...
I needed to have my mobile phone number to contact and be contacted by clients. Not being able to contact clients and be contacted by clients is catastrophic to the business of the [TT Trust].[3]

...
(c) By way of affidavit filed on 15 September 2025:
...
The [TT Trust] is a business owned by my partner, [Ms Karamazov]. I perform work for the business and invoice [TT Trust] for my services.

...
The removal of computing equipment has had severe consequences for [TT Trust], which is a digital business . The following issues have arisen:
Productivity: Myself and others have been unable to work effectively, as all operations rely on computers.
Presentations and Work: No laptops has meant there can be no client meetings with effective presenting, note taking and work.
App Development: The business has been unable to test and deploy app development projects due to the removal of Apple iPhones and iPads used for testing, development, and interaction with the Apple App Store.
Social Media Management: The business has lost access to social media accounts, many of which require the dedicated phone number on my personal phone. This has prevented us from responding to inbound messages, moderating groups, and managing accounts for both [TT Trust] and its clients. Social media management is a core service offered by [TT Trust].
Online Accounts: Many essential online accounts and cloud services cannot be accessed without my personal phone number ([...]).
Client Communication: My personal phone number, which has been a primary point of contact for years, has been unresponsive for months. This has caused significant backlash from contacts, damaging business relationships. While an interim phone was provided by the independent lawyer, it was ineffective as the prepaid plan stopped working after a week.
Two-Factor Authentication: I have been unable to access essential accounts, including LinkedIn and other social media platforms, due to the lack of access to my personal phone number.
Missed Opportunities: I was unable to participate in an [class action] with a deadline of [...] July, as I could not retrieve the required class action number without my primary phone. This represents a lost opportunity.
The prolonged retention of computers, phones, and related equipment has been disastrous for [TT Trust], resulting in severe loss of opportunities and income.

...
The search orders have severely impacted [TT Trust] and my family's income, to the point where we are struggling to afford basic necessities such as food and fuel.
I do not understand why [Mr HH] has been unable to complete his work, as copying the devices should suffice for any further investigation. Retaining the original devices for this extended period is unnecessary.

Requests
I urgently request the return of all computer and phone equipment to allow [TT Trust] to resume operations.[4]

...
(d) By way of affidavit filed on 5 February 2026:
...

16. [in response to an allegation by the wife of breach of the orders of 19 June 2025 by way of changing of passwords] ...the passwords were changed in the normal course of my engagement by the [TT Trust] in day-to-day business activities.

17. I reiterate that I have no interest in the [TT Trust] and that the work I do for the [TT Trust] does not form part of the property pool and that the [TT Trust] was not part of the orders.

...

Impact on the [TT Trust] business

39. In my affidavit sworn 15 September 2025, I informed the Court that the [TT Trust] is a business owned by my partner, [Ms Karamazov], and that I provide services to the business on an invoiced basis.

40. In that affidavit, I detailed the serious consequences of the execution of the search orders, including seizure of equipment and loss of access to systems.

41. The impact on the business has been significant and ongoing, resulting in lost income and lost opportunities.

42. I understand that my solicitor has placed the wife’s solicitors on notice of the continuing damage and costs being incurred by the business.

Confidentiality and privilege concerns

43. Granting unrestricted access to my email accounts would expose:

confidential customer correspondence, commercial information, and [TT Trust] intellectual property;

privileged communications relating to matters in which the [TT Trust] has been engaged by law firms on behalf of clients; ...

...

49. ...I reiterate that I perform work for the [TT Trust] and it is normal and appropriate to inform my solicitor of matters relating to business operations and security.

(e) By way of affidavit filed on 26 February 2026:
...
In her Affidavit of 4 July 2025, [Ms Karamazov], who is the trustee of the [TT Trust], deposed that money from the trust was taken and that she wants the money back from the Receiver.
As a contractor/employee of the [TT Trust] I know that the Digital Assets taken by the Receiver were the property of the [TT Trust].[5]

...
Ms Karamazov has deposed (by way of affidavit filed in the husband’s case on 4 July 2025) to the following:
...

5. I am a full time mother.

6. I have a discretionary trust called [TT Trust]. I attach the trust deed.

7. I am the trustee of that trust.

8. The trust creates software and advises clients on IT.

9. I was at the search on Tuesday 1 July 2025.
Money from the trust was taken, and I need that money to pay expenses, including staff and rent. I want that money back from the receiver.
[Mr Fujimori] [the husband] communicates with the trust’s customers and with the jobs for the team.[6]
The trust deed for the TT Trust, which was annexed to Ms Karamazov’s affidavit filed 4 July 2025, was tendered at the hearing of the joinder application.[7] That deed recorded that the husband was the sole appointor. He is also a beneficiary.
At the hearing, the husband’s solicitor tendered documents which collectively suggest that on 20 August 2025, he resigned as appointor of the TT Trust and was replaced with Ms Karamazov.[8] It would appear that this information was not disclosed to the wife. It was certainly not disclosed to the Court despite the following:
(a) the resignation purportedly having occurred prior to the filing by the husband of documents in response to the wife’s application to join the trustee of the TT Trust;

(b) the trust deed having previously been filed in the husband’s own case as an annexure to the affidavit of Ms Karamazov filed on 4 July 2025;

(c) the affidavit of the wife’s solicitor relied upon in support of the joinder application having referred specifically to the husband’s role as appointor of the TT Trust;[9]

(d) the husband having filed no less than three affidavits in which he deposed to matters relating to the TT Trust after the date of the purported resignation;[10] and

(e) the clear relevance of this development to both the overall proceedings and the present application.

This is a serious and concerning breach of the husband's disclosure obligations, which completely undermines the repeated submissions made on behalf of the husband that he has provided full disclosure with respect to the TT Trust.
The husband’s conduct, both in resigning from his role as appointor and in concealing the fact that he had done so from the wife and the Court in direct breach of his obligation of full and frank disclosure,[11] provides strong support for the allegations made by the wife with respect to the TT Trust and assists her joinder application. It is also a matter of significant concern to the Court. The husband's solicitor declined an express opportunity offered to make submissions about the timing of the variation.
As I have mentioned, the husband asserts that the TT Trust “is a business” (which I take to mean that he asserts that it is used to operate a business) for which he provides services on an “invoiced basis.” The husband has not so much as provided evidence of the name of the business he says is operated by the trust, much less adduced any documentary evidence to demonstrate its operations or its financial circumstances, notwithstanding his disclosure obligations. He has put before the Court none of the invoices referred to, nor any independent documents whatsoever to support his assertions.
The husband’s evidence in relation to the TT Trust is vague and entirely unhelpful and again, undermines the submissions repeatedly made on his behalf to the effect that he has provided full disclosure. That submission is simply inconsistent with the evidence and therefore incapable of acceptance.
The husband did not disclose to the Court the existence of the TT Trust, nor the work he now says he does for the trust, until 4 July 2025, despite the fact that the trust deed bears the date 12 January 2024, despite having put evidence before the Court with respect to his work and employment circumstances on 4 June 2025. This non-disclosure is, again, a matter of serious concern to the Court, and again, undermines the submissions made on behalf of the husband and lends support to the wife's allegations and application.
The evidence filed by the husband prior to the making of the orders of 19 June 2025 suggested variously that Ms Karamazov managed a business operated via a company of which he was a director, that she worked on a casual basis in hospitality and that he was providing her with financial support.
The wife relies on a number of matters, which it is suggested indicates overlap between the dealings and affairs of the husband and the TT Trust, including:
(a) Ms Karamazov's evidence that she is a full-time mother;

(b) what is said to have been a mistake in the evidence given by Ms Karamazov as to the nature of the assets of the trust which were seized pursuant to the orders of 19 June 2025;

(c) the alignment between the husband's skill set and the operations of the business that is not named and about which no disclosure has been provided but is said to be operated by the trust; and

(d) the husband's own evidence that the inability of clients to the trust to contact the trust following the seizure of his devices pursuant to the search order were, in his words, "catastrophic" to the business of the trust.

The wife also alleges that the husband has instructed his solicitors to communicate with the wife's solicitors in a manner in which the wife describes as “blurring the lines” between the husband and the TT Trust. For example, the wife's solicitor deposes that the husband's solicitor wrote to the wife’s solicitor on 16 December 2025, asserting that the business activities of the trust require protection, as an excuse for the independent forensic expert's access to certain accounts having been removed by way of a change of access credentials, notwithstanding the terms of the ongoing search order. The husband’s evidence given in response, that it is “normal and appropriate to inform [his] solicitor of matters relating to business operations and security” tends to support the very point raised by the wife in this regard.
The wife alleges that the husband is, or may be, directing Ms Karamazov in her capacity as trustee of the TT Trust, to take actions to “meddle with” the property of the parties. The evidence adduced by the husband in response has done nothing whatsoever to undermine that suggestion.
The wife also points to messages, copies of which were obtained as a result of the search order, suggesting that cryptocurrency has been transferred by Ms Karamazov to her brother in Country JJ, and transferred back to Australia in the form of cash, and to a lack of disclosure of evidence provided in relation to those transactions. These messages are said to undermine the suggestion that information could adequately be obtained by the wife, or the wife's position preserved, without orders being made directly against Ms Karamazov. In my view, they also support the extension of the concern about actions being taken with respect to property that is likely to be property of the parties by Ms Karamazov at the direction of the husband in her capacity as trustee to actions undertaken in her personal capacity and provide support for that aspect of the wife’s joinder application.
It is submitted in the husband's case outline that “a joinder must rely on evidence that the [TT Trust] has assets to be disclosed for the property pool.” Similar submissions were made this day on behalf of the husband. That is not an accurate statement of the law with respect to joinder.
Most of the submissions made on behalf of the husband this day focused on what he alleges are substantial difficulties with the evidence upon which the search order was based, his assertions that he has provided full cooperation with the search order, and his assertions that there is no more to be found by way of the search order.
These submissions, which may well go to what is ultimately found to be available for distribution between the husband and the wife, are of minimal significance to the question I am presently determining.
The same is true of submissions made on behalf of Ms Karamazov to the effect that there has been no demonstration of any wrongdoing on her behalf. The wife is not required to demonstrate any wrongdoing in order to establish any of the requirements for joinder.
It was submitted in the outline filed on behalf of the husband that the joinder application was without proper evidence. A similar submission was made on behalf of Ms Karamazov today. I do not agree. The evidence of the husband and Ms Karamazov themselves, to which I have already referred, provide ample evidence in support of the joinder application. For this reason, I reject the submissions made on behalf of Ms Karamazov today that the present application is a “witch-hunt,” “fishing,” or “just being suspicious.”
The joinder application is further supported by the reference in the husband's case outline today, to the TT Trust incurring “business and reputational damage” as a result of orders made by this Court, albeit that assertion is, as with all of the assertions made by or on behalf of the husband with respect to the vaguely described and unnamed business, unsupported by any documentary evidence whatsoever.
It was submitted on behalf of Ms Karamazov that the size of the asset pool renders the joinder disproportionate. The size of the asset pool is not yet known, is the subject of extensive and ongoing investigations, and is the major issue requiring determination in the proceedings, but that is not, in any event, the test for joinder.

RELEVANT LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Rule 3.01 of the Rules provides as follows:
3.01 Necessary parties

A person whose rights may be directly affected by an issue in a proceeding, and whose participation as a party is necessary for the court to determine all issues in dispute in the proceeding, must be included as a party to the proceeding.
The requirements for joinder in rule 3.01 are conjunctive. A person must be included as a party to the proceeding, if their rights may be directly affected by an issue in the proceeding and also their participation as a party is necessary for the court to determine all issues in dispute.[12]
If both aspects of the rule are made out, joinder is mandated.[13] The wife, as the applicant, bears the burden of demonstrating to the Court that both limbs of rule 3.01 are satisfied.
In general, as held by the High Court of Australia in John Alexander's Clubs Pty Ltd & White City Tennis Club Ltd,[14] where a court is invited to make or proposes to make orders directly affecting the rights and liabilities of a non-party, the non-party is a necessary party and ought to be joined. As a consequence, it has been said that the second limb, being necessity, adds little to the first.[15]

RELEVANT APPLICATIONS

The wife's application for final orders has not been particularised, and nor could it be, in circumstances where there remain substantial investigations underway as to the assets in the husband's control, and the true nature of the holdings and beneficial ownership of such assets.
By way of her Application in a Proceeding filed on 13 January 2026, the wife seeks orders against Ms Karamazov both personally and in her capacity as trustee of the TT Trust as follows:
Until further order: [Ms Karamazov] [sic] for the [TT Trust] be restrained and an injunction is hereby granted restraining them from transferring, encumbering or in any way dealing with the property of the [TT Trust], other than in the ordinary course of business.
The Orders dated 31 March 2021 be amended as follows:
5.1. to insert the words “The [TT Trust]” at the following locations therein:
5.1.1. After order 5.3, a new line creating item 5.4;

5.1.2. After order 11.5, a new line creating line item 11.6; and
5.2 To insert the words “and [Ms Karamazov] [sic]” at the following locations therein:
5.2.1. At order 5, first line after the word “Husband” and thereafter all male gendered pronouns be changed to plural non gendered pronouns (i.e. ‘they’);

5.2.2. At order 11, first line after the word “Husband” and thereafter all male gendered pronouns be changed to plural, non gendered pronouns (i.e. ‘they’).
...
The Husband in all capacities, [Ms Karamazov] and [Ms Karamazov] as trustee of the [TT Trust] to:
7.1. Forthwith yield up all passwords to all Onedrive accounts; and

7.2. Yield up all mobile phone providing access to two factor authentication (2FA).
Within twenty-one (21) days of the date of these orders, the Husband and [Ms Karamazov] personally and [Ms Karamazov] as trustee of the [TT Trust] provide the Wife with:

8.1. copies of all the documents as contemplated in rule 6.06 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth);

8.2. statements for all bank or credit union accounts (including loan, mortgage and credit cad [sic] accounts) in which the party has an interest for the period from the date of separation to the date of these Orders;

8.3. statements of all cryptocurrency or digital assets for the period from the date of separation to the date of these Orders which:
8.3.1. details the party’s acquisition or disposal of property for the period from 12 months prior to the date of separation to the date of these orders;

8.3.2. evidences any increase or reduction in the liabilities of the party for the period from separation to the date of these orders.
Until further order, the Husband and [Ms Karamazov] in any capacity be precluded from creating, establishing or otherwise setting up any:
9.1. companies;

9.2. trusts;

9.3. cryptocurrency exchanges;

9.4. cryptocurrency exchange accounts;

9.5. converting, transferring or disposing of any cryptocurrency; or

9.6. changing any login details, email addresses or passwords associated with any cryptocurrency exchanges or wallets;

9.7. sending any money overseas.
Orders 5 and 11 of the orders of 31 March 2021, being the orders which the wife seeks to vary, provide as follows:
That until further order the Husband be restrained, and an injunction is granted restraining him from selling, transferring, disposing, assigning, encumbering, charging or in any other way dealing with or authorising or permitting any corporation or trust or other entity of which he is a director or trustee to sell, transfer, dispose of, assign, encumber, charge or in any other way deal with any and all cryptocurrencies and any other asset in its ownership, possession or control and whether or through an exchange or through a wallet or through any other digital or electronic or other device in his capacity as director or other office-holder of any of the following corporations or as trustee of any of the following trusts or other office-holder in any corporation which acts as the trustee of such trust:
5.1. [B Holdings Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]);

5.2. [H Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]); and

5.3. [Fujimori Family Trust].
...
That subject to any other order made herein until further order the Husband be restrained, and an injunction is granted restraining him from selling, transferring, disposing, assigning, encumbering, charging or in any other way dealing with or authorising or permitting any corporation or other entity of which he is a director or other office-holder to sell, transfer, dispose of, assign, encumber, charge or in any other way deal with any or all cryptocurrencies and any other asset in his or its ownership, possession or control and whether on or through an exchange or on or through a wallet or on or through any other digital or electronic or other device, whether in his personal capacity or in his capacity as director or other office-holder of any of the following corporations or as trustee of any of the following trusts or other office-holder in any corporation which acts as the trustee of such trust save than in the ordinary course of business and/or the payment of reasonable living expenses:
11.1. [B Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]);

11.2. [B Group Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]);

11.3. [E Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]);

11.4. [Q Pty Ltd] (ACN [...]); and

11.5. [Fujimori Family Property Trust].
NOTING that the nature of the company [B Group Pty Ltd] is such that the company develops new products and methods of delivery and payment.
Furthermore, by Application in a Proceeding filed 12 January 2026, the wife seeks that the receivers appointed pursuant to the orders of 19 June 2025 be authorised to sell cryptocurrency currently held by them in relation to this matter to pay their invoice. The husband has not yet filed a response to that application, but his evidence (albeit unsupported by any substantiating documents) has been that the cryptocurrency held by the receiver forms part of the assets of the TT Trust.

DISCUSSION

Might the proposed third party’s rights be directly affected by an issue in the proceeding?

The Full Court of the Family Court of Australia confirmed in A Bank & Coleiro and Anor[16] that joinder does not require that an order be sought against the party proposed to be joined, and it has been recognised that a third person might be affected by an order though not directly the subject of it.[17] Nonetheless, the question of whether an order is sought against the party proposed to be joined has been described as a “critical consideration.”[18]
In the present matter, the wife seeks orders directly against Ms Karamazov and in relation to assets which are claimed by the husband to be assets of the TT Trust. The orders sought would plainly impose obligations and restrictions directly on Ms Karamazov both personally and in her capacity as trustee and would thereby directly affect her rights.

Is the third party’s participation necessary?

I now turn to consider whether Ms Karamazov's participation is necessary for the Court to determine all issues in dispute in these proceedings. In this context, "necessary" means something more than useful or expeditious.[19]
Generally, where a party seeks relief against another person, then that person should be named as a respondent.[20] Any person joined to a proceeding ought at that point be able to take advice on whether the facts pleaded, if established, would lead to a successful claim.[21]
To justify a joinder, sufficient facts must be asserted to demonstrate that, if proved, the law would support the relief sought.[22] The Court must also consider whether the applicant for joinder has a reasonable prospect of success in their claim against a third party.[23] That is, the case put against the third party must be sufficiently arguable to withstand a summary dismissal application,[24] noting that on a summary dismissal application the evidence of the party seeking joinder would be taken at its highest.[25]
To the extent that the wife's application against Ms Karamazov seeks orders for disclosure, this alone would be unlikely to be sufficient to justify her joinder. As the Full Court held in Wayne & Dillon and Anor,[26] if there are other available means of obtaining orders needed to enable the applicant for joinder to establish an identified case, such as subpoenas or a request for answers to specific questions, joinder is unlikely to be necessary in the relevant sense.
The fact that disclosure might more efficiently be obtained does not, of itself, provide an appropriate basis for joinder as a party to the proceedings.[27] Indeed, joinder for the sole purpose of obtaining disclosure has been held to be an improper purpose.[28] If that were all that was sought, I would agree with the submission made on behalf of Ms Karamazov that there are other means by which what is sought by the wife could be obtained. However, the relief sought by the wife in this matter insofar as it is sought against Ms Karamazov and/or in relation to the assets which, on the husband's case, belong to or affect the TT Trust, far exceeds orders as to disclosure.
The wife's position is that all or most of the digital assets the subject of the search and other orders are assets of the husband. The husband's evidence is that many of them are assets of the TT Trust or clients of the trust and ought not, therefore, be the subject of orders between the spouse parties, though those clients are not named, and there is no evidence to support that assertion. Even if the husband did succeed in establishing his claim, noting again that he has adduced no evidence to support it, there still remains a question as to the extent of his control over the assets of the trust, noting that he was until relevantly recently the appointor, and noting the contents of his evidence and that of Ms Karamazov to which I have referred.
The orders sought by the wife are directed to preservation of her position in relation to what, on her case, is property of the parties for the purposes of section 79 of the Act, pending what will, in light of the respective positions of the spouse parties, need to be a determination of the true nature of the ownership of those assets at trial. It is inconceivable that the wife's applications in this regard could fairly or properly be dealt with without the participation of Ms Karamazov in light of the evidence the husband has put before the Court.
The husband himself has repeatedly positioned the TT Trust as a barrier to relief sought by the wife and also to his provision of access to accounts and information required by the forensic expert in order to properly perform his role. The wife's solicitor alleges that he has used the trust to shield himself from compliance with the orders of the Court and to thwart the forensic expert's investigations.
The husband's solicitor relied on an email from the office of the solicitor for the independent lawyer appointed pursuant to the search order dated 3 March 2026 indicating that “nothing further is required at this stage” in order to deflect that allegation.[29] In my view, that email does not prove full compliance on behalf of the husband, and suggests merely that at that particular point in time, being some nine months after the search order was made, nothing in particular was being sought from him at that point.
In his affidavit filed in support of his response to the present application, the husband continued to assert that conduct on his part which is alleged to have impeded the forensic expert's investigation had been undertaken in the normal course of his engagement in the day-to-day business activities of the TT Trust.
He continues to assert that he has no interest in the trust, which contradicts other evidence filed on his behalf demonstrating that is a beneficiary, and he claims, yet has produced noting to establish, that the trust and the work he does for it are entirely separate to the matrimonial property dispute. He also continues to claim, but also continues to fail to adduce any documentary evidence to demonstrate, that the trust and the still unnamed business that is said to operate have been adversely impacted by the orders already made by the Court, which he says have resulted in unspecified and unproven lost income and unspecified and unproven lost opportunities.
Somewhat bizarrely, at paragraph 48 of his affidavit filed on 5 February 2026, the husband purported to defer to the legal representatives of the TT Trust, rather than responding to evidence adduced by the wife's solicitor about matters including communications received by them from the husband's solicitor on the husband's instructions, the import of earlier evidence given by the husband himself, and conduct undertaken by the husband.
The husband's willingness to defer to the representatives of the TT Trust in relation to matters raised involving his own conduct and his own financial circumstances provides strong support for the wife's allegation as to their interwoven affairs and therefore her joinder application.
The husband's evidence that the proceedings are having a direct impact on the TT Trust, his position that the trust is relevant to the extent to which he can or must provide access to the forensic expert and comply with Court orders in relation to the assets the subject of the expert's investigations, and his suggestion that the representatives of the trust would be called upon to answer matters raised in relation to communications sent by the husband's own solicitor all demonstrate the necessity for the participation of the trustee.
There is no basis for considering that the wife's applications lack reasonable prospects of success if her evidence were ultimately accepted. Indeed, it would appear at first blush that they are supported by much of the evidence adduced by the husband himself.

CONCLUSION

In light of these considerations, I am satisfied that Ms Karamazov's rights may be directly affected by an issue in a proceeding and that her participation is necessary, both her personal capacity and in her capacity as trustee of the TT Trust. Orders for her joinder will be made as sought by the wife.
